elements.sent-2411-001: Learning dynamical models from data is not only fundamental but also holds great promise for advancing principle discovery, time-series prediction, and controller design.
elements.sent-2411-002: Among various approaches, Gaussian Process State-Space Models (GPSSMs) have recently gained significant attention due to their combination of flexibility and interpretability.
elements.sent-2411-003: However, for online learning, the field lacks an efficient method suitable for scenarios where prior information regarding data distribution and model function is limited.
elements.sent-2411-004: To address this issue, this paper proposes a recursive GPSSM method with adaptive capabilities for both operating domains and Gaussian process (GP) hyperparameters.
elements.sent-2411-005: Specifically, we first utilize first-order linearization to derive a Bayesian update equation for the joint distribution between the system state and the GP model, enabling closed-form and domain-independent learning.
elements.sent-2411-006: Second, an online selection algorithm for inducing points is developed based on informative criteria to achieve lightweight learning.
elements.sent-2411-007: Third, to support online hyperparameter optimization, we recover historical measurement information from the current filtering distribution.
elements.sent-2411-008: Comprehensive evaluations on both synthetic and real-world datasets demonstrate the superior accuracy, computational efficiency, and adaptability of our method compared to state-of-the-art online GPSSM techniques.
elements.sent-2411-009: By summarizing historical information into the latent state, SSMs provide a concise representation of system dynamics and are widely applied in fields such as finance \cite{wu2014gaussian , neuroscience \cite{hu2024modeling , target tracking \cite{veiback2019learning , and control systems \cite{mchutchon2015nonlinear .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-010: Nevertheless, due to the complexity of real-world systems, establishing precise SSMs based on first principles is challenging.
elements.sent-2411-011: To achieve sufficiently accurate system models, extensive research has focused on data-driven SSM modeling \cite{rangapuram2018deep,gedon2021deep,frigolaBayesianTimeSeries2015 .}
elements.sent-2411-012: In the past decade, a novel SSM learning method based on the Gaussian process (GP), a nonparametric modeling technique, has been explored \cite{turner2010state,frigolaBayesianTimeSeries2015 .}
elements.sent-2411-013: This approach models the transition and measurement functions of SSMs as GPs, and is therefore referred to as Gaussian process state-space models (GPSSMs) \cite{frigolaBayesianTimeSeries2015 .}
elements.sent-2411-014: By employing GPs, GPSSMs offer flexible learning for the transition and measurement functions and provide prediction uncertainty, which is crucial for safety-critical applications \cite{fisac2018general .}
elements.sent-2411-015: To date, various GPSSM methods have been developed, including sample-based methods \cite{frigola2013bayesian,frigola2014variational,svensson2017flexible and variational inference (VI)-based methods \cite{eleftheriadis2017identification,doerrProbabilisticRecurrentStateSpace2018,Ialongo2019,lindinger2022laplace,fan2023free,Lin2024 .}}
elements.sent-2411-016: Among these, most studies focus solely on offline scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-017: However, in engineering practice, many systems experience non-stationary changes (e.g., drone propeller performance varies with battery levels), and there is the possibility of operating in out-of-distribution settings.
elements.sent-2411-018: This underscores the urgent need for an online learning method for GPSSMs, which is the focus of this paper.
elements.sent-2411-019: Facilitating online learning for GPSSMs presents significant challenges.
elements.sent-2411-020: Beyond the usual constraints associated with online learning—such as minimizing memory usage, reducing computational complexity, and preventing catastrophic forgetting—GPSSMs pose additional, unique difficulties.
elements.sent-2411-021: These challenges are detailed as follows.
elements.sent-2411-022: In GPSSMs, the system state is included in the input to the GP model, introducing inherent nonlinearity.
elements.sent-2411-023: This nonlinearity is intractable, which prevents exact inference through analytic methods.
elements.sent-2411-024: For standard Gaussian process regression (GPR), the training complexity scales cubically with the number of samples \cite{rasmussenGaussianProcessesMachine2008 .}
elements.sent-2411-025: In an online context, as new data arrives continuously, the computational cost of GPs increases rapidly, rendering inference intractable.
elements.sent-2411-026: Since the state is implicitly measured, learning the system model requires inferring the system state.
elements.sent-2411-027: However, state inference depends on the system model itself.
elements.sent-2411-028: As a result, there is a coupling between the state and the model, which increases the complexity of inference.
elements.sent-2411-029: Theoretically, since both are uncertain, they should be treated as latent variables and inferred simultaneously.
elements.sent-2411-030: GPSSMs involve several hyperparameters, such as GP kernel parameters, which impact the learning accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-031: In online settings, hyperparameter optimization presents two main challenges.
elements.sent-2411-032: First, due to memory efficiency considerations, historical measurements are not retained in online scenarios, depriving hyperparameter optimization of explicit information sources.
elements.sent-2411-033: Secondly, since the GP prior depends on kernel hyperparameters, tuning these parameters requires corresponding adjustments to the posterior distribution.
elements.sent-2411-034: Hence, the online GPSSM problem is quite challenging, and as a result, relevant research remains limited.
elements.sent-2411-035: Theoretically, implementing online GPSSMs involves Bayesian inference.
elements.sent-2411-036: Therefore, existing methods primarily rely on Bayesian filtering or online variational inference techniques, which can be classified into three categories:.
elements.sent-2411-038: This approach can jointly infer the system state and the GP model online, and its implementation involves two steps: first, parameterizing the GP with a limited number of parameters using inducing-point approximation \cite{veiback2019learning,Kullberg2020 , or spectral approximation \cite{Kullberg2021 ; and second, augmenting the GP model parameters into the state space and inferring the augmented state using the extended Kalman filter (EKF).}}
elements.sent-2411-039: Through the AKF-based framework, this method achieves recursive learning and offers advantages in learning speed and stability.
elements.sent-2411-040: However, there is a key limitation: the parameterization of the GP requires prior knowledge of the operating domain, specifically the location of inducing points or the range of basis functions in spectral approximation \cite{Kullberg2021 .}
elements.sent-2411-041: This drawback limits the approach's ability to adapt to the flexible distribution of online data.
elements.sent-2411-042: In addition, there is no feasible method for hyperparameter optimization for this approach.
elements.sent-2411-044: This approach is similar to the first, with the key difference being the use of the particle filter (PF) for the inference process.
elements.sent-2411-045: Since PF is inefficient at handling high-dimensional states, GP parameters are not augmented into the state space.
elements.sent-2411-046: Instead, each particle is assigned a GP, which is recursively learned using the particle state.
elements.sent-2411-047: This method can theoretically achieve precise inference for arbitrary observational distributions, provided the number of particles is sufficient.
elements.sent-2411-048: However, despite the inherent issues with PF, such as high computational cost and particle degeneracy \cite{doucet2009tutorial , it also requires prior knowledge of the operating domain.}
elements.sent-2411-050: This approach extends the offline variational inference method of GPSSMs by utilizing stochastic optimization techniques.
elements.sent-2411-051: It aims to retain many of the benefits of offline GPSSMs, such as a flexible operating domain and hyperparameter optimization capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-052: However, stochastic optimization assumes that the data subsampling process is uniformly random, which is not the case in most online situations.
elements.sent-2411-053: As a result, the learning process is driven by instantaneous data, leading to slow learning and catastrophic forgetting.
elements.sent-2411-054: In summary, the existing methods either lack the ability to tune the operating domains and hyperparameters, or they face issues with learning speed and convergence.
elements.sent-2411-055: To overcome the aforementioned shortcomings, a recursive learning algorithm for GPSSMs is proposed in this paper, capable of online adaptation to the operating domain and GP hyperparameters.
elements.sent-2411-056: The main contributions can be summarized as follows: Firstly, to implement a domain-independent recursive learning algorithm for GPSSMs, a novel Bayesian update equation is derived without pre-parameterizing the GP.
elements.sent-2411-057: In the derivation process, only mild linearization approximations are applied to achieve a closed-form solution, offering advantages in learning speed and stability.
elements.sent-2411-058: Through continuously expanding the inducing-point set, this method can accommodate real-time data distributions.
elements.sent-2411-059: Secondly, to address the computational cost issue caused by the increasing number of inducing points, an selection algorithm is developed with both addition and removal operations.
elements.sent-2411-060: To minimize accuracy loss, an optimal discarding equation and selection metric for inducing points are derived based on informative criteria.
elements.sent-2411-061: Thirdly, to alleviate the burden of offline hyperparameter tuning, an online GP hyperparameter optimization method is proposed.
elements.sent-2411-062: In this method, an approximate observation likelihood model is extracted from the obtained posterior distribution.
elements.sent-2411-063: Utilizing this likelihood model as the information source, the GP hyperparameters are optimized, and the posterior distribution can be adjusted as the hyperparameters change.
elements.sent-2411-064: Finally, we validate the effectiveness of the proposed method through two synthetic simulations and a real-world dataset.
elements.sent-2411-065: The experimental results demonstrate the superior performance of the proposed method in terms of learning accuracy, computational efficiency, and adaptive capacity for operating domains and hyperparameters.
elements.sent-2411-066: In this section, we first briefly provide the background of Gaussian processes (GPs) and then give a definition of Gaussian process state-space models (GPSSMs) and the associated online learning problem.
elements.sent-2411-067: Mathematically, GP is a probability distribution over function space, which can be employed for regression task.
elements.sent-2411-068: For learning a single-input function $f(\cdot)$, one can first place a GP prior on it: \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} f(x) \sim \mathcal{GP}\left(m_0(x), \boldsymbol{K}_0(x, x^\prime;\boldsymbol{\theta}) \right) \end{aligned}\end{equation} where $m_0(\cdot)$ denotes the prior mean function and $K_0(\cdot, \cdot)$ represents the prior covariance function (also known as kennel function), and $\boldsymbol{\theta}$ denotes the kernel hyperparameters.
elements.sent-2411-069: As a common setting, we assume zero-mean GP prior, namely, $m_0(\cdot) \equiv 0$.
elements.sent-2411-070: Then, given function values \( \boldsymbol{f _{\mathbf{X \) at input locations \( \mathbf{X \), the posterior distribution for test function values \( \boldsymbol{f _* \) is \cite{rasmussenGaussianProcessesMachine2008 : \begin{equation}\label{eq:GP_cond} \begin{aligned} p(\boldsymbol{f}_*|\boldsymbol{f}_{\mathbf{X}}) = \mathcal{N}( \boldsymbol{K}_{*X} \boldsymbol{K}_{XX}^{-1} \boldsymbol{f}_{\mathbf{X}}, \boldsymbol{K}_{**} - \boldsymbol{K}_{*X} \boldsymbol{K}_{XX}^{-1} \boldsymbol{K}_{X*} ) \end{aligned}\end{equation} Here, we use shorthand notation for the kernel matrix, e.g., \( \boldsymbol{K _{** \) denotes the auto-covariance of \( \boldsymbol{f _* \), and \( \boldsymbol{K _{* X \) the cross-covariance between \( \boldsymbol{f _* \) and \( \boldsymbol{f _{X \).}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.formula-2411-002: \begin{equation}\label{eq:GP_cond}  p(f_*|f_{X}) = N( K_{*X} K_{XX}^{-1} f_{X}, K_{**} - K_{*X} K_{XX}^{-1} K_{X*} ) \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-072: Indeed, GPs can be utilized to learn multi-output functions by employing an associated kernel.
elements.sent-2411-073: However, for simplicity, the elaboration of the proposed method is based on the single-output GP, and its extension to the multi-output case is straightforward and will be demonstrated in the experiments.
elements.subsection-2411-002: Online Gaussain Processs State Space Models
elements.sent-2411-074: In this paper, we consider the following discrete-time state-space model (SSM): \begin{equation}\label{eq:SSM} \begin{aligned} \boldsymbol{x}_{t+1} = \boldsymbol{F}(\boldsymbol{x}_t, f(\boldsymbol{x}_t)) + \boldsymbol{\omega}_f, \boldsymbol{\omega}_f \sim \mathcal N(\boldsymbol{0}, \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_f) \boldsymbol{y}_t = \boldsymbol{g}(\boldsymbol{x}_t) + \boldsymbol{\omega}_g, \boldsymbol{\omega}_g \sim \mathcal N(\boldsymbol{0}, \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_g) \end{aligned}\end{equation} where the vector $\boldsymbol{x \in \mathbb{R ^{n_x}$ and $\boldsymbol{y \in \mathbb{R ^{n_y}$ respectively denote the system state and measurement.}}}}
elements.formula-2411-003: \begin{equation}\label{eq:SSM}  x_{t+1} = F(x_t, f(x_t)) + \omega_f, \omega_f \sim \mathcal N(0, \Sigma_f) y_t = g(x_t) + \omega_g, \omega_g \sim \mathcal N(0, \Sigma_g) \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-075: The vectors $\boldsymbol{\omega _f$ and $\boldsymbol{\omega _g$ represent the process and measurement noise, with covariances $\boldsymbol{\Sigma _f$ and $\boldsymbol{\Sigma _g$, respectively.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-076: Additionally, the function $\boldsymbol{F : \mathbb{R ^{n_x \to \mathbb{R ^{n_x}$ denotes a known function structure for the transition model\footnote{ This structure of \( F(\cdot) \) can represent various models of interest, such as when only some dimensions of the transition model are unknown, or a discretized continuous-time transition model \( F(x_t, f(x_t)) = x_t + f(x_t) \Delta t \), where \( \Delta t \) is the time step and the unknown component is the time-derivative model \( \dot{x = f(x) \).}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-077: Though using a varying time step \( \Delta t \), the latter can be used for irregular measurement scenarios commonly occurring in biochemical sciences.
elements.sent-2411-078: To ensure identifiability, the measurement model $\boldsymbol{g : \mathbb{R ^{n_x \to \mathbb{R ^{n_y}$ is assumed to be known\footnote{ Even if the measurement model is unknown in practice, we can augment the measurement $\boldsymbol{y}$ into the state space and transform the problem into one with a known measurement model \cite{frigolaBayesianTimeSeries2015 .. .}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-079: To learn the unknown SSMs, GPSSMs place GP priors on the unknown function $f(\cdot)$, resulting in the following model \cite{frigolaBayesianTimeSeries2015 : \begin{subequations \begin{equation}x_0 \sim p(x_0) f(\cdot) \sim GP(m_0(\cdot), K_0(\cdot, \cdot);\theta) \label{eq:tran_density} x_{t+1} | x_{t},f \sim \mathcal N \left(x_t \, \big| \, F(x_t, f(x_t)), \, \Sigma_f \right) \label{eq:meas_density} y_t | x_t \sim \mathcal N \left(y_t \, \big| \, g(x_t), \, \Sigma_g \right)\end{equation} \end{subequations where $p(\boldsymbol{x _0)$ denotes a prior Gaussian distribution for the initial state $\boldsymbol{x _0$, and note that we use the notation $\boldsymbol{f}$ in \eqref{eq:tran_density to denote any collection of function values of interest, which includes $f(\boldsymbol{x _t)$.}}}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-081: In the online setting, measurements \( \boldsymbol{y \) arrive sequentially, and we assume that historical measurements are not retained for computational and storage reasons.}
elements.sent-2411-082: Given this streaming data setting, the inference task of online GPSSMs is to sequentially approximate the filtering distribution \( p(\boldsymbol{x _t, \boldsymbol{f |\boldsymbol{y _{1:t ) \), using the previous result \( p(\boldsymbol{x _{t-1 , \boldsymbol{f |\boldsymbol{y _{1:t-1 ) \) and the current measurement \( \boldsymbol{y _t \).}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-083: As illustrated in the introduction, several challenges exist in this task, including the nonlinearity and coupling involved, as well as the nonparametric nature of GPs and the hyperparameter optimization problem.
elements.sent-2411-084: To address these difficulties, a novel online GPSSM approach will be developed in the next two sections, with the ability to adapt to both operating domains and hyperparameters.
elements.section-2411-003: Bayesian Update Equation for Online GPSSMs
elements.sent-2411-085: This section derives a Bayesian update equation for online GPSSMs with minimal approximations to achieve recursive learning in an arbitrarily operating domain.
elements.sent-2411-086: The online inference of GPSSMs is essentially a filtering problem; therefore, most methods are based on filtering techniques, such as the extended Kalman filter (EKF) and particle filter (PF).
elements.sent-2411-087: Considering that the PF will exacerbate the computational cost issues due to GP and its particle degradation problem, we choose the EKF in this paper.
elements.sent-2411-088: The widely adopted EKF is a two-step version, which is more numerically stable \cite{oconnellNeuralFlyEnablesRapid2022 and can handle irregular measurements.}
elements.sent-2411-089: In each update, the EKF propagates the state distribution and then corrects it with the current measurement, which are known as the prediction (time update) and correction (measurement update) steps, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-090: In the context of GPSSMs, considering the coupling between the system state and the GP, the distribution to be propagated and corrected is the joint distribution of both.
elements.sent-2411-091: These two operations can be expressed by: \begin{equation}\label{eq:pred_step} p(x_{t+1}, f|y_{1:t}) = \int p(x_{t+1}|x_t, f) p(x_{t}, f|y_{1:t}) dx_t \label{eq:corr_step} p(x_{t+1}, f|y_{1:t+1}) = \dfrac{p(y_{t+1}|x_{t+1}) p(x_{t+1}, f|y_{1:t})}{p(y_{t+1}|y_{1:t})}\end{equation} where \( p(\boldsymbol{x _{t+1 |\boldsymbol{x _t, \boldsymbol{f ) \) and \( p(\boldsymbol{y _{t+1 |\boldsymbol{x _{t+1 ) \) are the transition and measurement densities as defined in \eqref{eq:GPSSM , \( p(\boldsymbol{x _t, \boldsymbol{f | \boldsymbol{y _{1:t ) \) denotes the joint posterior at time \( t \), and \( p(\boldsymbol{x _{t+1 , \boldsymbol{f | \boldsymbol{y _{1:t ) \) is the joint prior or predicted distribution at time \( t+1 \).}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-092: To evaluate the distribution in equations \eqref{eq:pred_step and \eqref{eq:corr_step , the key challenges are the nonparametric nature of the GP and the nonlinearity in the transition and measurement models.}}
elements.sent-2411-093: Existing methods \cite{Kullberg2020,veiback2019learning,Kullberg2021,Berntorp2021,zhaoStreamingVariationalMonte2023 typically first address the former by pre-parameterizing the GP into a fixed structure and then applying nonlinear filtering to handle the latter.}
elements.sent-2411-094: However, pre-parameterization limits the operating domain, thus reducing the algorithm's flexibility.
elements.sent-2411-095: To overcome this, we reverse the process: first handling the nonlinearity without any GP approximation, then addressing the nonparametric nature.
elements.sent-2411-096: We achieve the first procedure in the next subsection, resulting in an update equation with a factorized approximate distribution.
elements.sent-2411-097: To address the nonlinearity within the transition and measurement model, first-order linearization is applied in this subsection, whose result is the following theorem.
elements.sent-2411-098: By approximating the transition and measurement models with first-order linearization, the joint distribution between the state and GP obtained in each prediction and correction step follows the factorized form: \begin{equation}\label{eq:approx_dist0} \begin{aligned} q(\boldsymbol{x}_t, \boldsymbol{f}) = p(\boldsymbol{f}|\boldsymbol{u}) q(\boldsymbol{x}_t, \boldsymbol{u}) \end{aligned}\end{equation} where $\boldsymbol{u \triangleq \{ u_i \}_{i=1 ^{n_u}$ is a collection of a finite number of inducing points, \( p(\boldsymbol{f |\boldsymbol{u ) \) is the exact GP conditional distribution similar to \eqref{eq:GP_cond , and \( q(\boldsymbol{x _t, \boldsymbol{u ) \) is a Gaussian distribution.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-099: Given Theorem \ref{theorem1 , the approximate joint distribution remains Gaussian, which can be evaluated using closed-form update equations.}
elements.sent-2411-100: In the following, we will prove Theorem \ref{theorem1 and then derive these equations.}
elements.sent-2411-101: To facilitate the proof, we first introduce some notation regarding the approximate distributions \( q(\boldsymbol{x _t, \boldsymbol{u ) \) and \( q(\boldsymbol{x _t, \boldsymbol{f ) \).}}}}
elements.sent-2411-102: Specifically, these two distributions are expressed as: \begin{equation}\label{eq:approx_dist} \begin{aligned} q(\boldsymbol{x}_t, \boldsymbol{u}) = \mathcal{N}\left( \begin{bmatrix} \boldsymbol{x}_t \boldsymbol{u} \end{bmatrix} \Bigg | \begin{bmatrix} \boldsymbol{\mu}_t \boldsymbol{m}_{u} \end{bmatrix}, \begin{bmatrix} \boldsymbol{P}_t \boldsymbol{V}_{xu} \boldsymbol{V}_{xu}^T \boldsymbol{S}_{uu} \end{bmatrix} \right) q(\boldsymbol{x}_t, \boldsymbol{f}) = \mathcal{N}\left( \begin{bmatrix} \boldsymbol{x}_t \boldsymbol{f} \end{bmatrix} \Bigg | \begin{bmatrix} \boldsymbol{\mu}_t \boldsymbol{m}_{f} \end{bmatrix}, \begin{bmatrix} \boldsymbol{P}_t \boldsymbol{V}_{xf} \boldsymbol{V}_{xf}^T \boldsymbol{S}_{ff} \end{bmatrix} \right) \end{aligned}\end{equation} In this expression, the moments of the joint distribution are distinguished based on the state \( \boldsymbol{x _t \) and inducing points \( \boldsymbol{u \) or function values \( \boldsymbol{f \).}}}

elements.section-2411-004: Adaptation of Inducing points and Hyperparameters
elements.sent-2411-138: While the recursive learning algorithm for GPSSMs has been developed, two issues remain: the computational burden problem due to the non-parameteric nature of GP, and the hyperparameter online adaptation problem.

elements.subsection-2411-006: Dynamic Adjusting the Inducing Points Set
elements.sent-2411-140: As discussed in Section \ref{sec:update_eq , the computational complexity of the learning algorithm increases with the addition of inducing points.}
elements.sent-2411-141: This challenge is also encountered in the development of kernel-based online regression algorithms, such as Kernel Recursive Least Squares (KRLS) \cite{VanVaerenbergh2012 and Sparse Online Gaussian Processes (SOGP) \cite{csatoSparseLineGaussian2002,csatoGaussianProcessesIterative2002 .}}
elements.sent-2411-142: These two methods are, to some extent, equivalent and control computational cost by limiting the size of the inducing point set within a predefined budget \( M \).
elements.sent-2411-143: Specifically, this strategy is achieved through two operations: discarding the least important point when the set exceeds the budget \( M \), and adding only sufficiently novel points.
elements.sent-2411-144: To implement the discarding and adding operations, the following two key problems must be addressed: (1) how to remove a point with minimal accuracy loss, and (2) how to evaluate the importance or novelty of a point.
elements.sent-2411-145: In the following, we will address these two problems in the context of GPSSMs and develop an adjustment method for the inducing point set, thereby overcoming the computational burden issue.
elements.sent-2411-146: For convenience, let \( u_d \) denote the inducing point to be discarded, with index \( i_d \) in the point set, and let \( \boldsymbol{u _{l \) represent the remaining inducing points.}}

elements.sent-2411-177: However, as illustrated in the Introduction, the online setting presents two challenges for hyperparameter optimization: 1) the information source problem due to the lack of data retention, and 2) the coupling between GP hyperparameters and the posterior distribution.
elements.sent-2411-178: In this subsection, we will address these two challenges and propose an online GP hyperparameter optimization method for GPSSMs.

elements.sent-2411-195: This method reduces the burden of offline hyperparameter tuning and improves online learning accuracy by adjusting the GP prior to match the actual function characteristics.
elements.sent-2411-196: The online optimization method for GP hyperparameters most closely related to this paper is \cite{buiStreamingSparseGaussian2017 , an online GPR method.}
elements.sent-2411-197: In this method, the likelihood model is similarly recovered from the approximate posterior distribution, and it is used to optimize the location of inducing points and GP hyperparameters.
elements.sent-2411-198: Clearly, in GPSSMs, it is possible to optimize the inducing inputs in the same way.
elements.sent-2411-199: However, we do not pursue this approach because simultaneously optimizing the inducing inputs and hyperparameters would complicate the loss function and the adjustment equation for the posterior distribution.
elements.sent-2411-200: Additionally, we observe that in GPSSMs, due to the system states being implicitly measured, the difficulty of optimizing the inducing inputs increases.
elements.sent-2411-201: Therefore, we separate the optimization of inducing inputs and GP hyperparameters and determine the inducing-point set using a discretized selection method.
elements.sent-2411-202: In summary, we have addressed the four challenges outlined in the Introduction for implementing online GPSSMs.
elements.sent-2411-203: Specifically, first, nonlinearity and coupling are handled through first-order linearization and joint inference of the system state and GP, leading to a closed-form Bayesian update equation.
elements.sent-2411-204: Second, a dynamic inducing-point adjustment algorithm is developed to overcome the computational burden caused by the non-parametric nature of the GP.
elements.sent-2411-205: Third, we recover the likelihood model from the approximate posterior distribution and utilize it for the online optimization of GP hyperparameters, thus enhancing learning accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-206: Based on these solutions, we have developed an efficient online GPSSM method that can adapt to both the operating domain and GP hyperparameters.
elements.sent-2411-207: Due to its flexible recursive inference capability for GPSSMs, this method is referred to as the recursive GPSSM (RGPSSM), which is summarized in Algorithm \ref{alg:rgpssm .}
elements.algorithm-2411-001: \caption{Recursive Gaussian Process State Space Model (RGPSSM)}\label{alg:rgpssm} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \item[] Input: initial inducing points $\boldsymbol{u}$, threshold for adding points $\varepsilon_{tol}$, budget for the inducing-point set $M$. \REPEAT \STATE Assess the novelty $\gamma$ of the new point $f_t$ using \eqref{eq:gam}. \IF{$\gamma > \varepsilon_{tol}$} \STATE Add $f_t$ to the inducing-point set and propagate the moments using \eqref{eq:pred}. \ELSE \STATE Propagate the moments using \eqref{eq:pred1}. \ENDIF \IF{the number of inducing points $n_u > M$} \STATE Identify the least important point based on the score in \eqref{eq:score} and remove it using \eqref{eq:res_proj}. \ENDIF \IF{a new measurement $y_t$ is available} \STATE Correct the moments using \eqref{eq:corr}. \ENDIF \STATE Minimize $\mathcal{L}$ in \eqref{eq:loss} to update the GP hyperparameters $\boldsymbol{\theta}$ using the Adam optimizer \cite{kingma2014adam}, and subsequently update the moments using \eqref{eq:update_hp}. \UNTIL{operation ends} \end{algorithmic}
elements.sent-2411-208: In theory, Algorithm \ref{alg:rgpssm has the recursive learning capability for GPSSMs; however, as with the Kalman filter, there are numerical stability issues when implemented on a computer.}
elements.sent-2411-209: Specifically, the algorithm requires the propagation of the covariance matrix \( \boldsymbol{\Sigma _t \) of the augmented state \( \boldsymbol{X _t \), which can easily lose positive definiteness due to the accumulation of rounding errors.}}
elements.sent-2411-210: To address this problem, a stable implementation based on Cholesky factorization is derived, as detailed in Appendix \ref{app:stable .}
elements.sent-2411-211: Then, considering the Cholesky factorization, along with the matrix inversion and determinant operations involved in Algorithm \ref{alg:rgpssm , the computational complexity of a single update for RGPSSM is limited to \( \mathcal{O (M^3) \).}}
elements.sent-2411-212: In addition, RGPSSM can be viewed as a generalization that encompasses the pre-parameterized AKF-based online GPSSM method using inducing points \cite{Kullberg2020 and the kernel-based online regression method KRLS \cite{VanVaerenbergh2012 and SOGP \cite{csatoGaussianProcessesIterative2002 as special cases.}}}
elements.sent-2411-213: More precisely, when the RGPSSM is initialized with pre-selected inducing points and does not update them by setting \( \varepsilon_{\text{tol = +\infty \), it is similar to the pre-parameterized AKF-based method.}}
elements.sent-2411-214: Additionally, if the transition and measurement functions of GPSSMs are taken as \( x_{t = f(\boldsymbol{c _{t ) \) and \( y_t = x_t \), where \( \boldsymbol{c _t \) and \( y_t \) represent function input and output, RGPSSM is similar to KRLS and SOGP.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-215: Moreover, benefiting from limited assumptions and the two-step inference format, the RGPSSM is suitable for arbitrary kernel functions and irregular measurement intervals or measurement losses.
elements.sent-2411-216: Since it belongs to the AKF framework, theoretically, existing adaptive Kalman filter methods may be seamlessly integrated to adapt to the process and measurement noise covariance within the RGPSSM.
elements.sent-2411-217: In the next section, we will validate the effectiveness and advantages of RGPSSM through several experiments.

elements.sent-2411-241: To further evaluate RGPSSM's ability to online adapt GP hyperparameters and inducing points, it is tested on the synthetic wing rock dynamics learning task.
elements.sent-2411-242: In aerospace engineering, wing rock dynamics can cause roll oscillations in slender delta-winged aircraft, posing a significant threat to flight safety.
elements.sent-2411-243: To address this issue, most approaches rely on online learning of wing rock dynamics \cite{GirishChowdhary2015 .}
elements.sent-2411-244: The associated dynamical system can be expressed by the following continuous-time SSM: \begin{equation}\label{eq:wr_sys} \begin{aligned} \dot \theta = p \dot p = L_{\delta_a} \delta_a + \Delta(\theta, p) \end{aligned}\end{equation} where \( \theta, p \in \mathbb{R \) denote the roll angle and roll rate, respectively.}
elements.sent-2411-245: As illustrated in \eqref{eq:wr_sys , the dynamics model consists of a known part \( L_{\delta_a \delta_a \), where $\delta_a$ is the aileron control input and \( L_{\delta_a = 3 \), and an unknown uncertainty model \( \Delta(\theta, p) \).}}}
elements.sent-2411-246: The uncertainty model used in the simulation is taken from \cite{GirishChowdhary2015 , namely: \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \Delta(\theta,p) = W_0 + W_1 \theta + W_2 p \, + W_3 \vert \theta \vert p + W_4 \vert p \vert p + W_5 \theta^2 \end{aligned}\end{equation} where $W_0=0.8, W_1=0.2314, W_2=0.6918, W_3=-0.6245, W_4=0.0095, W_5=0.0214$.}

elements.sent-2411-247: To further demonstrate the learning capacity of the proposed method under sparse sensor conditions, the measurement is limited to the roll angle \( \theta \), which is corrupted by Gaussian white noise with a standard deviation of 0.2 degrees.
elements.sent-2411-248: To apply the proposed method to learn wing rock dynamics, we specify a GP prior on the uncertainty model \( \Delta(\theta, p) \) and then apply Algorithm \ref{alg:rgpssm to the discretized-time form of \eqref{eq:wr_sys .}}

elements.sent-2411-274: In this paper, we propose a novel recursive learning method, RGPSSM, for online inference in Gaussian Process State Space Models (GPSSMs).
elements.sent-2411-275: First, to enable learning in any operating domain, we derive a two-step Bayesian update equation that does not rely on any GP approximation.
elements.sent-2411-276: The derivation incorporates a mild first-order linearization to address the inherent nonlinearity within GPSSMs.
elements.sent-2411-277: Second, to mitigate the computational burden imposed by the nonparametric nature of GPs, we introduce a dynamic inducing-point set adjustment algorithm, ensuring computational efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-278: Third, to eliminate the burden for offline tuning of GP hyperparameters, we develop an online optimization method that extracts measurement information from the approximate posterior distribution.
elements.sent-2411-279: Through extensive evaluations on a diverse set of real and synthetic datasets, we demonstrate the superior learning performance and adaptability of the proposed method.
elements.sent-2411-280: In the future, research may focus on improving the accuracy of the first-order linearization by using techniques such as the iterative Kalman filter, extending the method to handle non-Gaussian data likelihoods, and adapting it to process and measurement noise covariances, as well as time-varying dynamics.. {\appendices.}

elements.sent-2411-325: Therefore, GPs have gradually become a powerful tool for various applications.
elements.sent-2411-326: Building on the aforementioned advances, GPs have been naturally developed for the modeling of dynamical systems.
elements.sent-2411-327: In the context of SSM learning, GPs can be employed to model the transition and measurement functions, resulting in what are known as Gaussian process state-space models (GPSSMs) \cite {frigolaBayesianTimeSeries2015 .}
elements.sent-2411-328: By employing GPs, GPSSMs offer flexible learning for the dynamical system and provide prediction uncertainty, which is crucial for safety-critical applications \cite{fisac2018general .}
elements.sent-2411-329: To date, various GPSSM methods have been developed, including sample-based methods \cite{frigola2013bayesian,frigola2014variational,svensson2017flexible,fan2023free and variational inference (VI)-based methods \cite{eleftheriadis2017identification,doerrProbabilisticRecurrentStateSpace2018,Ialongo2019,lindinger2022laplace,Lin2024 .}}

elements.sent-2411-436: A schematic diagram of the algorithmic workflow is shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:alg_flow .}
elements.algorithm-2411-002: \caption{Recursive Gaussian Process State Space Model (RGPSSM)}\label{alg:rgpssm} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \item[] Input: initial inducing points $\bm{u}$, threshold for adding points $\varepsilon_{tol}$, budget for the inducing-point set $M$. \REPEAT \STATE Assess the novelty $\gamma$ of the new point $f_t$ using \eqref{eq:gam}. \IF{$\gamma > \varepsilon_{tol}$} \STATE Add $f_t$ to the inducing-point set and propagate the moments using \eqref{eq:pred}. \ELSE \STATE Propagate the moments using \eqref{eq:pred1}. \ENDIF \IF{the number of inducing points $n_u > M$} \STATE Identify the least important point based on the score in \eqref{eq:score} and remove it using \eqref{eq:res_proj}. \ENDIF \IF{a new measurement $y_t$ is available} \STATE Correct the moments using \eqref{eq:corr}. \ENDIF \STATE Minimize $\mathcal{L}$ in \eqref{eq:loss} to update the GP hyperparameters $\bm{\theta}$ using the Adam optimizer \cite{kingma2014adam}, and subsequently update the moments using \eqref{eq:update_hp}. \UNTIL{operation ends} \end{algorithmic}
elements.figure-2411-007: \includegraphics[width=0.99\textwidth]{fig/algorithm.pdf} \caption{Schematic diagram of the RGPSSM algorithmic workflow.} \label{fig:alg_flow}

elements.section-2411-012: Extension to Multi-output GP and Discussion
elements.sent-2411-440: This section extends the single-output GP version of the RGPSSM method to the multi-output GP case, and systematically analyzes the computational complexity, generalization, and potential of the proposed method.
elements.sent-2411-441: In numerous practical applications, the unknown function to be learned within SSMs exhibits multiple outputs, represented as $\bm{f(\cdot) : \mathbb R^{d_x \to \mathbb R^{d_f}$ where $d_f > 1$.}}
elements.sent-2411-442: To accommodate this multi-output nature, the GP modeling for $\bm{f(\cdot)}$ must be extended to a multi-output form, necessitating a modification of the kernel function $\bm{K _0(\cdot, \cdot)$ to yield a $d_f \times d_f$ matrix output.}
elements.sent-2411-443: For this purpose, we employ a straightforward multi-output kernel formulation, expressed as: \begin{equation}\label{eq:multi_output_kernel} \begin{aligned} \bm{K}_0(\cdot, \cdot) = {K}_0(\cdot, \cdot)\, \bm{\Xi} \end{aligned}\end{equation} where ${K _0(\cdot, \cdot)$ in the RHS represents the single-output kernel, and $\bm{\Xi}$ denotes the signal covariance between each function output dimension.}
elements.sent-2411-444: Based on this multi-output kernel function, the kernel matrix has a Kronecker product structure, i.e., $\bm{K _0(\mathcal{X _1, \mathcal{X _2) = {K _0(\mathcal{X _1, \mathcal{X _2) \otimes \bm{\Xi}$.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-445: This structure indicates that the $(i,j)$-th $d_f \times d_f$ block of the kernel matrix $\bm{K _0(\mathcal{X _1, \mathcal{X _2)$ is the prior covariance between function values $\bm{f (x_i)$ and $\bm{f (x_j)$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-446: Therefore, the only modification from the single-output GP version of RGPSSM to the multi-output GP version is to convert scalar elements in related vectors or matrices into corresponding blocks, which are presented in the following.
elements.sent-2411-447: Firstly, the moments of joint distribution $q(\bm x, \bm u)$ including mean $\bm m_{u}$, cross-covariance $\bm V_{x u}$ and auto-covariance $\bm S_{uu}$ have new definitions.. Specifically,.
elements.sent-2411-448: The $i$-th column block (with shape $d_f \times 1$) of column vector $\bm m_{u}$ represents the mean of the inducing point $\bm u_i$.
elements.sent-2411-449: The $i$-th row block (with shape $d_x \times d_f$) of matrix $\bm V_{x u}$ represents the cross-covariance between state $\bm x$ and the inducing point $\bm u_i$.
elements.sent-2411-450: The $(i, j)$-th block (with shape $d_f \times d_f$) of matrix $\bm S_{uu}$ represents the cross-covariance between the inducing points $\bm u_i$ and $\bm u_j$.
elements.sent-2411-451: Secondly, among the inducing point management, the calculation of the score $s_d$ and novelty $\gamma$ require updates.
elements.sent-2411-452: For the evaluation of the score $s_d$ in \eqref{eq:score , the modifications only involve updating the definitions of several quantities:.}
elements.sent-2411-453: For the criterion of adding points, since $\bm\gamma$ in \eqref{eq:gam is now a matrix, the criterion is modified to $\mathrm{tr (\bm\gamma) > \varepsilon_{tol}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-454: Additionally, when adding or removing inducing points, the updates to the moments are performed in a block-wise manner based on the new definitions.
elements.sent-2411-455: Overall, the extension to the multi-output GP version is straightforward, with modifications involving the kernel function, the definitions of some vectors and matrices, and the evaluation criteria for inducing points management.
elements.sent-2411-456: It is evident that the dimensions of statistical moments will scale with the dimension of GP output, thus impacting the computational complexity, which is analyzed in the next subsection.
elements.sent-2411-457: The computational complexity of RGPSSM is analyzed across its various algorithmic steps in the following table, where a detailed comparison between the standard and Cholesky-based implementations of RGPSSM is presented.
elements.sent-2411-458: The analysis is based on the multi-output GP version of RGPSSM.
elements.figure-2411-008: \caption{Computational complexity comparison between standard and Cholesky versions of RGPSSM} \begin{tabular}{lcccc} Algorithm step Standard RGPSSM Involved equations Cholesky RGPSSM Involved equations GP prediction $\mathcal{O}\left( (d_f M)^2 \right)$ \eqref{eq:qf} $\mathcal{O}\left( (d_f M)^2 \right)$ \eqref{eq:qf} Moment propagation $\mathcal{O}\left( (d_x + d_f M)^2 \right)$ \eqref{eq:pred}, \eqref{eq:pred1} $\mathcal{O}\left( (d_x + d_f M)^3 \right)$ \eqref{eq:qr0}-\eqref{eq:chol_pred2} Inducing point removal $\mathcal{O}\left( (d_x + d_f M)^3 \right)$ \eqref{eq:score} $\mathcal{O}\left( (d_x + d_f M)^3 \right)$ \eqref{eq:score}, \eqref{eq:chol_discard} Moment correction $\mathcal{O}\left( (d_x + d_f M)^2 \right)$ \eqref{eq:corr} $\mathcal{O}\left( (d_x + d_f M)^2 \right)$ \eqref{eq:chol_post} Hyperparameter update $\mathcal{O}\left( (d_x + d_f M)^3 \right)$ \eqref{eq:loss}, \eqref{eq:update_hp} $\mathcal{O}\left( (d_x + d_f M)^3 \right)$ \eqref{eq:loss}, \eqref{eq:update_hp}, \eqref{eq:chol_hp} Total complexity $\mathcal{O}\left( (d_x + d_f M)^3 \right)$ - $\mathcal{O}\left( (d_x + d_f M)^3 \right)$ - \end{tabular} \label{tab:complexity}

elements.sent-2411-462: Although the Cholesky version incurs higher computational costs in certain algorithmic steps, it offers superior numerical stability.
elements.sent-2411-463: Therefore, we recommend the Cholesky version for general applications, which is adopted throughout all experimental evaluations.

elements.sent-2411-501: The experimental results from multiple simulations with different random seeds are summarized in Table \ref{tab:sysID .}
elements.sent-2411-502: As shown in the table, although online learning is challenging, the proposed method achieves the best prediction accuracy on three datasets.
elements.sent-2411-503: This superior performance can be attributed to the fact that while offline methods can utilize the full dataset for repeated training, their learning accuracy is often limited by various factors, such as local optima, vanishing or exploding gradients during SSM/RNN training \cite{pascanu2013difficulty , or insufficient expressiveness of the variational distribution (e.g., the mean-field assumption in PRSSM \cite{doerrProbabilisticRecurrentStateSpace2018 , Gaussian parametric constraints over the state and inducing points posterior in VCDT \cite{Ialongo2019 , or using the filtering distribution to approximate the smoothing distribution in EnVI \cite{Lin2024 ).}}}}
elements.sent-2411-504: However, RGPSSM fully considers the coupling between the system state and the GP, with only the linearization approximation limiting its learning capability.
elements.sent-2411-505: Therefore, in some cases, the online learning performance of the proposed method is comparable to that of offline methods.
elements.sent-2411-506: Of course, due to the linearization approximation, the proposed method may have limitations in highly nonlinear scenarios, which could potentially be addressed by integrating advanced nonlinear filtering techniques.
elements.sent-2411-507: Additionally, we mention that the results of online learning have the potential to provide effective initializations for offline learning, thereby improving both training speed and accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-508: This is an interesting direction that can be further explored in future work.
elements.sent-2411-509: First, to enable learning in any operating domain, we derive a two-step Bayesian update equation that does not rely on any GP approximation, which provides a new solution for the nonlinearity and nonparametric issues of GPSSMs.
elements.sent-2411-510: Second, to mitigate the computational burden imposed by the nonparametric nature of GPs, we introduce a dynamic inducing-point set adjustment algorithm to ensure computational efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-511: Third, to eliminate the need for careful tuning of GP hyperparameters at initialization and to enhance online learning accuracy, we develop an online optimization method that extracts measurement information from the approximate posterior distribution.
elements.sent-2411-512: Fourth, we derive a Cholesky version of RGPSSM in detail to further improve numerical stability, and also present the extension of RGPSSM to the multi-output GP case.
elements.sent-2411-513: Fifth, we analyze the computational complexity of the proposed method and demonstrate that it generalizes several existing approaches, as well as highlight its strong potential for further extensions.
elements.sent-2411-514: Finally, through extensive evaluations on a diverse set of real and synthetic datasets, we demonstrate the superior learning performance and adaptability of the proposed method.
elements.sent-2411-515: In the future, research may focus on enhancing nonlinear filtering accuracy, improving computational efficiency, scaling it to high-dimensional problems, and adapting it to process and measurement noise covariances, as well as time-varying dynamics.

elements.sent-2411-576: State-space models (SSMs), also known as hidden Markov models (HMMs), describe the evolution and observation processes of latent states through a state transition model and a measurement model.
elements.sent-2411-577: By summarizing historical information into the latent state, SSMs provide a concise representation of system dynamics and are widely applied in fields such as motion planning \cite{he2025novel , target tracking \cite{kang2026stochastic,zhang2023group , and control systems design \cite{breschi2023data,kessler2026design,da2025fault,2025Experience .}}}
elements.sent-2411-578: To achieve sufficiently accurate system models, extensive research has focused on data-driven SSM modeling \cite{Berntorp2021,rangapuram2018deep,gedon2021deep,frigolaBayesianTimeSeries2015,zheng2025model .}
elements.sent-2411-579: Mathematically, a GP is a probability distribution over the function space, providing a flexible and probabilistic framework for modeling unknown functions.
elements.sent-2411-580: GPs not only yield predictions but also quantify uncertainty, making them widely used in machine learning \cite{rasmussenGaussianProcessesMachine2008 and system identification \cite{pillonetto2022regularization,care2023kernel .}}
elements.sent-2411-581: For example, the early variational GPSSM method proposed by Frigola et al. \cite{frigola2014variational combines variational Bayes and sequential Monte Carlo (SMC) to learn the GPSSM by iteratively updating the GP and the state trajectory.}
elements.sent-2411-582: In engineering practice, many systems experience non-stationary changes or varies cross tasks, and there is the possibility of operating in out-of-distribution settings.
elements.sent-2411-583: In addition to the typical constraints of online learning, such as minimizing memory usage, reducing computational complexity, and preventing catastrophic forgetting, GPSSMs introduce further unique difficulties.
elements.sent-2411-584: These challenges are described as follows.
elements.sent-2411-585: Since the state is implicitly measured in general, learning the system model requires estimating the system state.
elements.sent-2411-586: However, state estimation depends on the system model itself.
elements.sent-2411-587: In summary, the online GPSSM problem is quite challenging, and as a result, relevant research remains limited.
elements.sent-2411-588: To address the aforementioned shortcomings, this paper proposes a recursive learning algorithm for GPSSMs that is capable of online adaptation to the operating domain and GP hyperparameters.

elements.sent-2411-692: In summary, by recovering an approximate likelihood model from the current filtering distribution, this subsection derives the objective function and posterior update equation for online hyperparameter optimization.
elements.sent-2411-693: Clearly, in the context of GPSSMs, it is possible to optimize the inducing inputs in the same way.

elements.sent-2411-695: Compared to existing online GPSSM methods, the proposed method is the first to achieve recursive inference for GPSSMs with the ability to adapt to both the operating domain and GP hyperparameters.

elements.algorithm-2411-003: \caption{Recursive Gaussian Process State Space Model (RGPSSM)} \label{alg:rgpssm} \begin{footnotesize} 1: Input: initial inducing points $\bm{u}$, threshold for adding points $\varepsilon_{tol}$, budget for the inducing-point set $M$. 2: Repeat until operation ends: 3: \quad Assess the novelty $\gamma$ of the new point $f_t$ using \eqref{eq:gam}. 4: \quad If $\gamma > \varepsilon_{tol}$ then 5: \qquad 0.8\linewidth{Add $f_t$ to the inducing-point set and propagate the moments using \eqref{eq:pred}.} 6: \quad Else 7: \qquad 0.8\linewidth{Propagate the moments using \eqref{eq:pred1}.} 8: \quad If the number of inducing points $n_u > M$ then 9: \qquad 0.8\linewidth{Identify the least important point based on the score in \eqref{eq:score} and remove it using \eqref{eq:res_proj}.} 10: \quad If a new measurement $y_t$ is available then 11: \qquad 0.8\linewidth{Correct the moments using \eqref{eq:corr}.} 12: \quad 0.8\linewidth{Minimize $\mathcal{L}$ in \eqref{eq:loss} to update the GP hyperparameters $\bm{\theta}$ using the Adam optimizer \cite{kingma2014adam}, and subsequently update the moments using \eqref{eq:update_hp}.} \end{footnotesize}

elements.sent-2411-738: Firstly, to derive the moments of the approximate joint distribution $q_{\mathrm{new (\bm{x _t, \bm{f )$, we can utilize the following result: \begin{equation}\label{eq:pno} \begin{aligned} \dfrac{p(\bm{u};\bm{\theta}_{\mathrm{new}})} {p(\bm{u};\bm{\theta}_{\mathrm{old}})} = \dfrac{\mathcal{N}(\bm{u}|\bm{0}, \bm{K}_{uu}^{\mathrm{new}})} {\mathcal{N}(\bm{u}|\bm{0}, \bm{K}_{uu}^{\mathrm{old}}) } = Z \mathcal{N}(\bm{u}|\bm{0}, \Delta \bm{K}) = Z \mathcal{N}(\bm{0}|\bm{u}, \Delta \bm{K}) \end{aligned}\end{equation} where \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \Delta \bm{K} = [(\bm{K}_{uu}^{\mathrm{new}})^{-1} - (\bm{K}_{uu}^{\mathrm{old}})^{-1}]^{-1} Z \propto \dfrac{\vert \bm{K}_{uu}^{\mathrm{old}} \vert^{1/2} \vert \Delta \bm{K} \vert^{1/2}}{\vert \bm{K}_{uu}^{\mathrm{new}} \vert^{1/2}} \end{aligned}\end{equation} Note that, the above equations hold only in a mathematical sense and does not have probabilistic meaning, as \(\Delta \bm{K}\) may not be positive definite.}}}}
